narrative.springFall: May through June and September through early October are Berlin at its most generous. Temperatures settle into a comfortable 15–22°C, biergartens and canal-side terraces hum into the evening, and the city's vast parks—Tiergarten, Tempelhofer Feld, Volkspark Friedrichshain—turn lush and unhurried. Spring brings the Berlin Gallery Weekend and the first long bike rides along the Spree; fall delivers golden light on the Reichstag dome, the Berlin Marathon, and harvest menus across Mitte and Neukölln. If you're planning your first visit, aim for late May or mid-September—long days, mild evenings, and crowds that haven't peaked.
narrative.summer: July and August bring the longest days—daylight stretching past 9:30pm, open-air clubs running until sunrise, and lake culture in full swing at Wannsee, Schlachtensee, and Müggelsee. Outdoor cinema in Hasenheide, lakeside swimming, and the legendary Berlin nightlife define the season. Trade-offs: rising temperatures (often 28–32°C) inside non-air-conditioned U-Bahn carriages, peak hotel prices, and Tempelhofer Feld baking in the afternoon sun. Berliners themselves often escape to Brandenburg lakes on weekends. Book club entry strategies, start sightseeing early, and embrace the city's late, late nights.
narrative.winter: November through February reveals Berlin's introspective side. Temperatures hover between -2 and 5°C, with short days and a grey palette that the city wears well. Christmas markets fill Gendarmenmarkt, Charlottenburg Palace, and dozens of neighborhood squares with Glühwein and bratwurst from late November. Museum Island feels nearly private, the techno scene runs warmer than ever, and cozy Kneipen become essential evening anchors. Hotel rates drop significantly. This is the best season for culture-focused travelers who want to spend long afternoons inside the Pergamon, the Gemäldegalerie, or the Berlinische Galerie without queues.
narrative.march: Berlin's slow awakening. Temperatures climb toward 8–12°C, the first hesitant tables appear outside Kreuzberg cafés, and locals reclaim the parks the moment a sliver of sun appears. Daylight lengthens noticeably, and the gallery and theatre seasons hit full stride before tourists arrive. This shoulder season offers excellent value—rates are still low, museums are still quiet, and the city feels genuinely local. Pack layers and accept some rain; Berlin springs are unpredictable. Perfect for travelers who want the city's depth without summer's crowds or winter's chill.

seasonalNeighborhoods.reason: leafy streets, Sunday flea markets, and easy café-to-park rhythm
canal-side beers, open-air clubs, and late-night street food
